title: Western NPC Archetypes
description: The people of the frontier — settlers, lawmen, outlaws, gamblers, drifters, and the full range of humanity that moves through a territory where law is new and the land is older than anyone's claim to it.

entries.result: A trail-worn sheriff who's been in the county fifteen years, wins arguments by outlasting people, and has a rule about not drawing first that he hasn't broken in a decade.
A gambler with a clean suit and a memory for every hand she's been dealt in the last three years. She cheats, but only against people who are cheating.
A cattle baron who built his spread from a single cow and a dishonest land claim in 1869 and has been defending both ever since.
A traveling medicine show proprietor who is genuinely skilled with herb medicine, genuinely dishonest about the bottles, and genuinely useful in a crisis.
A deputy so new that his badge is still bright, his gun hand is steady, and his understanding of the job is completely theoretical.
A retired outlaw trying to ranch quietly who is consistently contacted by people who haven't received the memo about the retirement.
A land office clerk who knows where every fraud in the county's deeds is buried and has been collecting that knowledge carefully for eight years.
A blacksmith who fixes anything, talks to everyone, charges fair prices, and has an opinion on every dispute in the county based on who owes her and who pays on time.
A drifter who has been drifting in the same direction for four years, toward something, at a pace that suggests he'll get there when he gets there.
A frontier judge who travels a circuit three counties wide, is meticulous about process, is honest, and is occasionally threatened in ways he doesn't find particularly effective.
A widower rancher who runs the operation well but has been making increasingly bad business decisions since his wife died and doesn't connect the two things.
A young woman who came west as a mail-order bride, arrived to find the situation different from described, and has been making her own arrangements since.
A bank manager whose bank is the most stable institution in three counties, which is a fact he's aware of and is working very hard not to take advantage of.
A scout who knows the territory better than anyone alive, charges accordingly, and won't cross certain areas for any price.
A reformed gunfighter teaching school, which is going fine except that everyone in town is waiting for something to go wrong and he can feel it.
A Mexican vaquero who's worked this range for thirty years, has formal claim to nothing, and knows the land better than the man who owns it on paper.
A Pinkerton agent who is either investigating something real or being used to intimidate someone legitimate, and may not know which.
A ranch cook who keeps no accounts, forgets nothing, and is owed favors by everyone who's eaten at her fire.
A frontier preacher who holds services wherever there are people, isn't particularly denominational about it, and is genuinely trying to help.
A mine foreman who knows his mine is dangerous, knows his company knows it, and is trying to figure out what he can do about it before someone dies.
A young outlaw who got into this by covering for someone else, has been covering for that person ever since, and is running out of cover.
A territorial representative who is corrupt in small ways, honest in large ones, and perpetually surprised by how often those two things conflict.
An old mountain man who comes into town twice a year, trades fair, says little, and returns to a location in the mountains that nobody's been able to follow him to.
A saloon owner who runs the most neutral ground in town — no sides taken, all information available for the right price — and has survived three previous owners of her building.
A horse trader with an eye for animals and a flexibility around provenance that makes him very useful and somewhat problematic.
A newspaper editor who has been printing accurate stories about the wrong people for two years and has become increasingly hard to frighten.
A woman rancher who inherited the operation from her father, has run it profitably for eight years, and is regularly underestimated by men who need to be corrected about this.
A town drunk who was a surveyor, remembers every survey he did, and is occasionally sober enough to be asked about them.
A bounty hunter who brings her quarry in alive out of a principled belief in the court system that is tested about once a week.
A telegraph operator who processes every message in the county and has the corresponding awareness of everything happening and the discipline to never discuss it.
A company man — railroad, land company, mining concern — who is doing what he's paid to do and knows it's making things worse and is still doing it.
A Civil War veteran who came west to outrun the war and found that the west had its own version of everything he was trying to leave.
A frontier doctor who practices out of a converted supply wagon, charges what people can pay, and has more surgical experience than any doctor east of the Mississippi.
A cattle drive trail boss who runs a tight operation, treats his crew fairly, and is currently carrying someone else's problem in his herd.
A local fixer — no official role, knows everyone — who arranges things, mediates disputes, and collects favors like other people collect debts.
A Wells Fargo agent who has been robbed twice on this route and is making the third run personally to resolve the question of how the robbers know the schedule.
A homesteader's wife who manages the books, the crops, the relations with neighbors, and the negotiations with buyers, while her husband gets the credit.
A hired gun who's gotten old enough to be careful, which makes him more dangerous, not less.
A former slave who homesteaded in the territory when it opened, has a valid claim, runs a successful operation, and has been dealing with interference since the first year.
A stagecoach driver who has been running the same route for twelve years and knows every rock on it.
A missionary who arrived with intentions and stayed because of complications and has since developed a nuanced view of whose help is actually wanted.
A young man trying to prove himself in ways that consistently put other people at risk.
A former Cavalry sergeant who left the Army over a matter of principle and is living with that decision in the territory where the principle was tested.
A cantina owner who is the most reliable source of accurate information in the region and is not paid enough for the risk this represents.
A sheepherder whose flock has been grazing disputed range for years, who knows the law is against him, and who has been staying out of it quietly while he builds a case.
A tracker who can follow anyone, will work for anyone who pays, and keeps a private ledger of every job and outcome that he has never shown to anyone.
A stage agent at a waystation who sees every traveler on this route, remembers faces, and has a story about someone who stopped here that is more important than it sounds.
A land speculator who is ahead of the settlement wave, buying cheap and intending to sell dear, and who is occasionally wrong about which direction the wave is going.
A bilingual merchant — English and Spanish — who operates as an informal broker between communities and whose neutrality is calculated and genuine in equal measure.
A frontier midwife who has delivered half the children in three counties, knows family histories better than any courthouse, and is owed more than she'll ever collect.
A young law student practicing at a frontier firm who has more idealism and legal knowledge than practical experience and is about to get a great deal of the latter.
A former sheriff who was run out of his previous county on charges that were technically valid and practically a frame-up, now working as a hand and watching.
A general store owner who extended credit to everyone in a bad year and now holds paper on half the county and doesn't want to collect.
A Chinese railroad laborer who stayed after the railroad moved on, built a successful laundry, and is subjected to restrictions that don't apply to anyone else in town.
A prospector who's been working the same creek for three years, is convinced there's something there, and may be right in a way he hasn't understood yet.
A land company agent who believes in what the company is doing, genuinely, and hasn't yet seen what it costs the people on the other side of the transaction.
A saloon pianist who plays every night, listens all night, and has been keeping a journal of what she hears for four years.
A veteran ranger who operates alone, in a jurisdiction that's technically not his, on a case that technically closed six months ago, because he doesn't believe the close.
A feed store owner who is the logistical center of the agricultural economy and has a clear view of which operations are healthy and which aren't based on what they're ordering.
A man who was lynched and survived and has been waiting patiently for the legal situation to develop to a point where he can make a formal complaint.
A contract drover who moves other people's cattle for a flat rate and has moved enough cattle to know which herds are carrying brands that don't match the paperwork.

A frontier fort commander who is maintaining peace between three entities who each believe they have exclusive claims to the same land, using a combination of tact, authority, and deliberately selective enforcement.

A woman who left back east with a secret, established a new identity in the territory, and has been living that identity successfully for seven years until someone from her previous life arrived on the stage.
A gunsmith who knows every gun in the county, who owns them, and what condition they're in — which is a form of intelligence that rarely gets identified as such.
A man with a grudge twenty years old who has been patient enough that the target no longer considers it an active threat.
A frontier school teacher who came for one term, stayed for nine years, and knows every family in the region through their children.
A union organizer who has arrived in a mining town and is trying to do something that the mine owners consider sufficiently threatening to respond to.
A barber who is the primary source of social information in town, having heard everything that matters in the last decade, and who has a view on all of it.
A stage robber who has retired, genuinely, and is now being accused of a robbery she didn't commit, which is fair in a cosmic sense and completely inconvenient in a legal one.
An irrigation engineer who arrived to build a canal system, has finished, and has opinions about how the water rights have been allocated that nobody wants to hear.
A frontier banker who is also the primary debt holder of the main criminal enterprise in the territory and has been trying to extract himself from that relationship for two years.
A man who survived a massacre, the only one who did, who has been working through whether to talk about it ever since.
A hotel keeper who has the same philosophy about neutrality as the best saloon owners, applied to a clientele that includes travelers with competing interests.
A cattle brand inspector who knows every legitimate brand in three territories and can identify forged ones on sight — a skill that has made him several enemies.
A man who was a significant person in another state and is now nobody here and prefers it, completely, and is going to remain that way unless someone digs.
A frontier photographer who travels the territory documenting it, whose images are evidence of things that people would prefer not to have documented.
A land company lawyer who is so good at the law that he's begun to see problems with some of his clients' positions that he hasn't raised yet.
A deputy who enforces the law selectively based on a personal moral code that is more consistent than the law he's supposed to be enforcing.
A woman who runs a boarding house, knows her guests better than she lets on, and has been providing a safe stopping point for certain categories of travelers for six years.
A scout who works both sides of any conflict he can, not for money, but because he believes the best outcome comes from having influence with everyone.
A mule skinner — drives freight — who covers more of the territory than anyone, knows the roads in all conditions, and has been witness to more than he's been asked about.
An old rancher who won't sell, can't be intimidated, has survived worse than the current situation, and has one friend who he hasn't introduced anyone to.
A young woman who was raised on the frontier and is better at most frontier skills than most men, which is a continuous source of surprise to men who've just met her.
A land company surveyor who has been fudging survey lines to benefit the company and has reached the limit of how far he's willing to go and is not sure what happens next.
A former Texas Ranger who operates in other territories based on a flexible interpretation of his authority that he's never been formally tested on.
A mine company safety inspector who is the only person in the operation who tells the truth about the mine's condition and is ignored with remarkable consistency.
A man who is being asked to do something criminal by someone with leverage, who is looking for a way out that doesn't end with him in the ground or in a cell.
A cattle drive cook with thirty years of experience who is the de facto morale officer, therapist, and weather forecaster for every outfit she rides with.
A prospector's widow who inherited his claim, which is producing nothing, and his debt, which is substantial, and has been working both problems with more success than anyone expected.
A stage line owner who is being squeezed by the railroad and is considering options that his drivers don't know about yet.
A land agent who has been skimming from clients for four years, has covered his tracks thoroughly, and has a competitor who suspects and is close.
A man who served time for a crime he committed, came out clean, and is being followed by an assumption of guilt that makes the clean impossible to maintain.
A veteran cattle hand who has seen every version of this industry and has stopped being surprised by any of it and started being useful to people who are still surprised.
A frontier judge's clerk who knows everything about the cases, has an opinion on all of them, and has been building leverage quietly for five years.
A man who came to the territory to die quietly and keeps getting involved in other people's problems in ways that suggest he changed his mind about the dying part.
A woman who runs a freight operation with four wagons and seven workers, is the largest independent freight carrier in the region, and is being offered prices that are designed to drive her out.
A deserter who's built a legitimate life under a new name for eight years and has just been recognized by an Army officer who hasn't decided what to do about it yet.
A tribal elder who is engaged in negotiations with the territorial government and believes, correctly, that the outcome will depend entirely on whether the agreement is honored after it's signed.
A well-known outlaw who has been operating in the territory for a decade and whose legend is larger than his actual crimes, which is both useful and a problem when people test the legend.

A lawman who has been in the territory long enough to know that the law and justice don't always go the same direction, and is at the point in his career where he has to decide which one he's working for.
